title: Differentiable Volumetric Rendering: Learning Implicit 3D Representations without 3D Supervision
problem: Learning-based 3D reconstruction usually depends on accurate 3D ground truth, which is expensive or unavailable for many real-world objects and scenes.
Differentiable rendering from 2D images had mostly been tied to voxels or meshes, so methods inherited discretization artifacts, memory growth with resolution, or fixed-template topology limits.
Implicit representations avoid discretizing geometry, but prior occupancy and texture models were typically trained with 3D supervision rather than RGB images, masks, or sparse depth.
For an implicit surface, the rendered intersection point changes with network parameters, so backpropagating an image loss requires a way to differentiate the predicted surface depth.
solution: The paper introduces Differentiable Volumetric Rendering (DVR), which renders an occupancy network and texture field by ray casting to the first isosurface intersection and evaluating color there (5b).
It represents shape as a continuous occupancy function and appearance as a continuous texture field, optionally conditioned on an image encoder latent code for single-view reconstruction **4a**.
The central step is to treat the surface hit as an implicit equation on a camera ray (5c) and use implicit differentiation to obtain an analytic gradient for surface depth **4c**.

Training samples image pixels and applies RGB, optional depth, freespace, and occupancy losses so object masks and 2D images can supervise both visible texture and missing or spurious geometry **4d**.
Because the depth-gradient formula depends only on derivatives at the final surface point, DVR avoids storing all volumetric samples from ray marching for the backward pass (5a).
key_concepts: Occupancy as geometry: the object surface is the level set f_theta(p, z) = tau, so geometry can be extracted at arbitrary resolution while training keeps a constant-size implicit function **4a**.
Texture on the surface: the texture field predicts RGB at 3D points, but rendering uses it only at the ray-surface hit, coupling appearance supervision directly to the learned shape **4b**.

Surface depth as the differentiable bottleneck: once the hit point is written as r(hat d), the difficult part of differentiable rendering becomes differentiating hat d rather than differentiating a rasterizer **4c**.

Implicit differentiation removes the need to unroll ray marching: the forward pass can search for the first occupancy transition, while the backward pass uses local derivatives of f_theta at the final hit point.

Mask-aware losses separate three pixel cases: valid surface hits receive photometric supervision, outside-mask rays remove false geometry with freespace loss, and inside-mask misses are encouraged to create occupancy **4d**.

variables.meaning: occupancy network that maps a 3D point and latent code to occupancy probability
texture field that maps a 3D point and latent code to RGB color
3D point in object or world coordinates
latent descriptor used to condition single-view shape and texture
shared neural network parameters
camera center for the current view
ray direction from the camera through pixel u
predicted surface depth along the ray
predicted intersection point with the implicit surface
occupancy threshold defining the isosurface
rendered RGB prediction at pixel u
change in predicted surface depth caused by changing network parameters
spatial derivative of occupancy at the predicted surface point
parameter derivative of occupancy at the predicted surface point
ray direction used to convert depth changes into 3D point changes
sampled pixels inside the object mask where the renderer predicts a finite surface depth
image feature function, implemented with RGB values and optionally image gradients
observed supervision image
image rendered from the implicit shape and texture model
ground truth depth for pixel u when depth supervision is available
predicted surface depth for pixel u

visuals.caption: Overview of differentiable volumetric rendering for implicit shape and texture representations
visuals.description: Shows that an analytic gradient of surface depth with respect to network parameters lets RGB image losses train an implicit 3D representation.

visuals.caption: Differentiable volumetric rendering pipeline
visuals.description: Depicts pixel sampling, ray-based occupancy evaluation, surface-depth prediction, texture-field evaluation, image/depth losses, and optional image conditioning.

visuals.caption: Ray notation for occupancy and texture rendering
visuals.description: Defines the camera ray, the first isosurface intersection, and the texture lookup used to render a pixel color.
